speaker: interviewer
text: This is take number two. The date is September the 5th, 1996. My name is Myra Slotnick and I'm interviewing Harold Blitzer in Las Vegas, Nevada. Harold, I wanted to go back to your school days in Berlin and I wanted you to tell me what your experiences were with anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can tell you this. The first two years was 19-- I think I went to school in 1931 or '32 my first year. So the first two years, I didn't know about, there was no anti-Semitism. Then we-- I transferred to another school in 1933 and for the first couple months, nothing happened. Then one day, our teacher came in in a brown uniform, SA. And I was sitting in the middle and I was the only Jew, by the way, in that class. He pointed me to the last seat in the back, you will sit over there now. All of a sudden, I was known as the Jew. Up to that time, we weren't-- people didn't pay any attention to because I don't think as a kid I looked much Jewish. You know, I had dark hair but I wasn't-- so had many German stock kids that were my age.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wasn't recognizable as a Jew. I didn't have a big nose like the newspapers at that time made the Jews out to be and-- and I didn't wear a yarmulke like, you know, I was-- so-- so it was rare. So all of a sudden, I was known as the Jew. So what happened, in between I got pushed around by the kids. You know, kids can be very cruel. And-- and people hit me. I remember one time the guy stood in front of me, he says, I'm going to hit you now because you know why? Because you can't hit me back. I hit him back after he hit me. Anyway, I gave him a shot, you know, because-- and he start crying. So it was a big stinking school. All of a sudden, the father came in and that's when-- when they took me out of that class and put me in a Jewish school. And to this day, I'm saying, why wasn't that a signal for my parents to get out of Germany? I mean, they should have known at that time-- this was '34, '35. My mother had a brother in the United States who could have been-- well, you can't talk about spilled milk. So the other thing I-- I-- I-- was-- I belonged to [INAUDIBLE], the sports club, as a soccer player. And the occasion for me to play soccer was so rare because at the German-- if the Germans decided to close down the-- the-- the stadium for a day and it-- it really wasn't-- I mean, it wasn't really a stadium.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was one of the worst places. It only had to goal posts and-- and there was some grass in it. And you know, there was no maintenance whatsoever. And we were never-- hardly ever allowed to play. And-- and many times, I used to smuggle myself into these pools, you know, swimming pools. And you know, you have to take a shower first. And I was circumcised so it wasn't-- so I had to always, like, walk through with the towel in front of me so nobody could spot me till I put my trunks on and jump in the pool. And then you couldn't go to a movie. And we didn't have a radio anymore, they were impounded. And we didn't have-- well, cars we never had anyway, so it didn't-- didn't-- was no big deal. But it-- it started little by little getting worse. You couldn't-- you weren't allowed to go to a museum, you weren't allowed to go to a-- to a play, you weren't allowed to go to a movie, you weren't allowed to go into a cafe house. My father occasionally years ago always took me with him once in a-- in a while so I could have a piece of cake and-- and my hot chocolate. All of a sudden, that was out, you know. And-- and then, of course, I imagine we didn't have the money anymore to-- to go to these places, even if we could. So little by little, it was actually become impossible, much more for a kid, and it was for-- like you mentioned before, the Germans thought it was a great thing with the Nuremberg laws, the German Jews. I-- I don't think if you had any-- that was the worst thing in the world. That should have been a signal for every Jew to get out of Europe or get out of Germany. And at that time, that was possible. [DRINKS] My father opened up a store in 1937 again. Now, that was stupid. I-- I-- I told him that after the war. I actually [INAUDIBLE] he was still alive. So-- so that's basically, I felt anti-Semitism all around. I was very unhappy, I couldn't play, there was no places to go. I used to love to go skating, ice skating. All of a sudden, you couldn't go there anymore. And things like that, what kids like to do was all forbidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Passover in your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Passover was very beautiful, it was. Because many times, my-- my grandfather, and I got a picture of him, he used to come and visit us from Poland. And he conducted a service, he was a very religious person. And we had a traditional Passover with the second set of dishes and-- and-- and we'd clean all the bread out and went through the whole thing. And-- and we had really, really-- to this day I know my [INAUDIBLE]. I know it because I used to practice it. God forbid, my grandfather would-- would catch me. And I used to know it by heart, you know. So because that was-- I was the youngest, I had to say it, you know. But beautiful. And then one of my uncles used to come there. And it's kind of interesting because he worked for my other uncle, who had a tailor-- clothing factory. And he was a bachelor and since was always going with Gentile girls, he was not allowed to go to my other uncle for Passover so he would always come-- because she said, you can come, but you've got to leave the girl-- the shikse behind. And so he always came. We always had room for him. He came there with his girlfriend and-- and she was a very nice lady. And-- and unfortunately, he did not survive. And so-- so we had a beautiful Passover. It was beautiful. We even-- we celebrated Rosh Hashanah. We went to the-- you know, I'm talking about 1935, 1936, you know. My father with that high hat and that dark tailcoat. And-- and we had fairly good seats. I remember Yom Kippur we had locks, we had-- where the books were locked in. And he always brought that sandwich for me. After 12 o'clock I could eat on Yom Kippur that sandwich, but I had to go way out from the synagogue. And so we had-- I-- I-- [INAUDIBLE] a typical Jewish home, you know, a modern Jewish home. We didn't keep kosher because we didn't-- we had the store downstairs which was not kosher. And I remember when my father opened up the second store, you know, and I overheard a conversation. He says, nothing is going to happen to us because we got our best customers are the SA. We had some big shots coming in because we made the finest liver pate in Berlin and we had-- my father used to go to Poland to buy the geese and the chickens because Poland had the quality of-- of poultry. And so he always was under the impression nothing can happen to him, like so many other Jews in Germany. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was your father's family in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was in the Austrian Army. Don't forget, when my father was born, he was born outside of Krakow, Poland. At that time when he was born, it was Austria, Austro-Hungarian Empire. So in 1912, two years before the war, he was drafted. And when he was ready for discharge, the-- the-- the other war started. So he spent six years in an Austrian regiment. And he got discharged in 1918. He was wounded a couple of times. He showed me once in a while his-- well, he didn't talk that much about it. And-- and I believe he came to Germany in 1919, right after-- after the war. Now, we had some family who lived in-- in the, right on the border there, and that's where he used to buy his stuff, in [INAUDIBLE], which is-- which is-- right in-- it's a border city in-- on the Polish side, which at one time was German or something like that, you know. So-- so that's where he-- because his family was in the wholesale business with poultry and things like that. And so my mother was born in Krakow. And they-- they-- they were married forever and forever. I remember that, you know. You know, from day one, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened when you were on the farm, what circumstances make you leave the farm in Denmark.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't leave the farm because I wanted to leave the farm. I was picked up in 1943 in the night of October 1st, 1943. And-- and-- about-- later on, maybe 20 minutes later, we went to this guy, Henry's farm, picked him up too. And there was Danish-- Danish people on the truck who evidently were part of the SS. And I never forget and-- and I'll tell you something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been thinking about that so much in the-- especially in the last year or so. I've been-- I says-- what happened was one of the SS men, the Danish SS men says to me and Henry and the-- they didn't-- they couldn't pull into the yard with the truck, you know. so they there were standing, you know, it was like an enclosed, you know, the typical farms were sort of like surrounded with an opening, right? So they couldn't pull in because they figured they couldn't turn them around. So he says, you and you, get him. Go over and get this guy, right? So we go, we run over. And the guy, we get him out of bed. He says, what are you, crazy? I'm going to-- I ain't going. And he jumps out a window and takes off. I look at Henry and we were so brainwashed that time with the Germans that we went back to the goddamned truck. And many years I've said to myself, my god, I would have-- I wouldn't have had a happy death in the gas chambers, I'd be thinking about I could have run away and I didn't. Because after we-- many years later-- a couple of years later when we were in Sweden, we met the guy and he got over to Sweden. But we were so scared we had no-- you've got to understand something. Where were we going to go? We had no contact with anybody, we-- we didn't know anybody in the underground. We figured that if they catch us, they're going to shoot us or something like that. That's that was the typical way of doing it. And whoever figured we were going to go into a place where they're going to kill you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody believed it, that it was possible, right? So I've been thinking about that episode for many, many years. And especially the last year, you says, you could have got away. Why didn't you? Why didn't you? I didn't have enough guts to do it. How old were you, Harold?

speaker: interviewee
text: 15, 16, you know, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me what happened after that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So now a couple of hours later, we get to where they did all their pickup and there was a couple of places they didn't find the people, right, but not that many. And so we had, like, a full truck already, you know. There was, like, maybe 25 or 30 people there, you know. And so by the late afternoon, we meet the other trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had a total of 150 people and there was like 10 cars there waiting at the railroad. So they split us up in two-- in two groups. They were based on all-- there were 8,000 Jews living in Denmark. And they were-- they were expecting this has never happened before, that anybody ran away, right? We didn't-- we were not-- we didn't even know what the hell was going on. So they divided us into three groups, 50 apiece. They put us in three cars. And believe it or not, 50 was crowded because I-- recently, I went the museum in Washington. I went into one of those cars. I said, I can't believe 50 people spent four nights-- you know, four nights in a place like that. Tell me about the car.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This was a cattle car and it had a bucket in the back there and no-- no water, no nothing, just-- so we were loaded on and then the train took off, you know. And it wasn't much of a fast train, but I know we went-- we-- we could see we were going south, you know. We weren't going to any place-- so we went over the Danish border, which was not really a border at that time, it was strictly manned by a-- German people because they still didn't let German people into Denmark. They didn't want anybody to go to Denmark and buy-- smuggle things in, you. Know so there was a border there, you know. So-- and it was-- one side was German, the other side was a Danish office-- Danish customs officer. So we went through that and all of a sudden, we're in Germany, you know. And I know we passed in the outskirts of Hamburg and we were going south, kept on going south. And then many times, we pulled over and they don't open the goddamn thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the second-- we were two days already and two nights when we got out, finally opened the door, emptied the buckets, right, and get yourself some water. But we didn't have a-- we didn't have a place to put water. I had rubber boots, right. Being on a farm, I had rubber boots. So we decided to use the two boots as a water container, right? So now I take the boots off and somebody goes out to-- to pour the water in them and they come back with the empty buckets and-- and the-- and the water, you know, for 50 people. So we each got a zip of it, you know, like-- and it was the finest, taste like wine. We were so thirsty. You can imagine how, you know, we had-- you know, if you would have had a bucket, they would have let us pull up a bucket, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so four days later, we arrive in a place called Theresienstadt. I had never heard of a place in my life, you know. And Theresienstadt was built many, many years ago as a fortress for Maria Teresa, the Austrian queen, therefore the name Theresienstadt, city-- city of Theresa. And it was-- it was built like a typical garrison city. It had these big barracks which were not barracks in our sense of a way like we do in the United States Army. These were big buildings made out of-- build like brick and everything. And they used to house maybe 1,500 soldiers or something like that, you know. Well, all of a sudden, we first get-- as we come in, we go into a reception area. It was a separate little camp. At that time, we were supposed to get the physical examination, you know, we're supposed to turn in our money and values and things like that, you know. And I didn't have-- I think I had, like, six kroner, which was like $0.40, on me. That's all I had.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and they take away our luggage and things like that. And that took, like, you had the examination-- bend over, you know, you get undressed, you bend over and cough so they can see what you can hide things. But we were not that sophisticated to hide. We didn't have anything to hide anyway, right? We had a little food left, you know, because one thing we did have the-- the guard told us when we were picked up, tell the farmer to give you some food for a couple of days, right? So we got-- and something terrible happened on that train ride. I opened my package. There was a piece of bacon in there and it was moldy. And this one guy says to me, you can't eat that. I says, what do you mean? I said, if I cut them off? No, no. He said, that's going to poison you. He says, throw it out the window. Throw it out through that little window. I cursed this guy for two and a half-- for two years. I said, why did he make me do that? I threw it out the-- you know, that little barbed wire window you can look through, you know. That's the way we checked where we were going, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so in the late afternoon-- in the meantime, we had nothing to eat yet, right? So we were assigned to a certain barracks, you know. So there was no room in the barracks, so they put him on a loft. And no blankets, no nothing. And I saw in the corner there, there was some straw mattresses. So I grabbed two, one to lay on and one to sleep. And I woke up about a couple of hours later, I was covered with lice and fleas and, like, I was scratching myself. I was going crazy, right? So-- so I got rid of the mattress and I sat down just to be around. And-- and there were-- I mean, once the fleas get into your clothes, it's all over, you know. And I'm very allergic to things like that. And so I could hardly wait for the next morning, you know. So we-- now, we're going to the mess-- we're going-- the rations for the day was-- in the morning was like what they call imitation coffee, you know, with the-- but it was hot. We had no place to put it. We didn't have a dish. Every three days, you got a piece of bread which had to last you three days, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: We missed the ration because at that-- the one we supposed to get was issued. The bread we was going to go the next day. We had to go the next day. So now the most important thing was happening. We have to get a dish. So now the other prisoners come over. We've got a dish, but here's for you. We've got-- we're going to sell you a dish.

speaker: interviewee
text: How much? Well, three-- one guy wanted the full ration of bread. I says, no. I'll give you half. So he settled for half. So I had a dish at least. So that lunch, I had what they call the soup, the vegetable soup. Now, if you were lucky, the guy gave it to you from the bottom. If you weren't lucky, you'd get it from the top. It was-- you know, how he felt. And there was nothing in it. On the bottom there was maybe a potato or something like that, you know, something-- a potato peel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Potatoes, there's no such thing. We got the peels. So at least we had something. And I was so hungry the next-- and that night we're getting-- so I'm finding out what the menu was. Three-- three nights a week at night, all you do is got coffee. The other four nights could be either soup or occasionally they had-- you were splitting a-- they used to have tins of liver pate, real-- the worst, I-- I would say. I don't know, they came maybe from the First World War, I don't know. And they were divided into two pieces. You had to split that. One live pate for two people, one liver paste for two people. And you had to open the can, cut it in half and you got half. Now, at that time I never had any bread left over because I-- the minute I got ration I saw guys making three lines, you know, one today. I did the same thing, but by the end of the day, it was gone. I had-- I was so hungry. So for three days, I had nothing to eat besides that. So hunger was the worst things. Now all of a sudden, I'm getting sick. Take me to the-- I'm really-- I mean, I'm breaking out and everything. And all this garbage on me. So they take me to the hospital. Turns out, I have diphtheria. So we had an unbelievable doctor there. I don't-- I don't remember his name because he was a professor-- he was a professor of children diseases comes over to my bed and says, look, this is-- you get the last piece of injection here. It's all I got left. He gives me the shot and I get better, right. So it took about 10 days for me to get discharged from the hospital and I-- now I'm clean, you know, we didn't have any bugs in the hospital. And there was a place to once in a while you'd get a shower and things like that-- cold water, of course. But I was used to cold water, that's all we-- that's all we could do in Denmark. We didn't have-- never had warm water. We had a cold water shower there. Take a shower in the wintertime. So-- so now I'm out of the hospital. So now I'm getting assigned to a duty. So I-- and I was in good shape at that time, you know, coming from a farm. They assigned me to the wood chopping group, which a bunch of Czechs. They were built like closets, that's-- I mean, really not a single German there. They spoke Czech and you better start learning how to-- and so we went out every day outside the camp and chopped down wood not for us, for the SS. Chopped trees, cut them off and split them and things like that. And I remember one day, it-- in-- God, it was like the end of October, something was-- all of a sudden, you had that-- it was a beautiful warm day. And we had nothing to eat. I had nothing to eat because I had no bread left. All these other Czechs had bread, you know. They knew-- they had been in the camp before. So I'm looking at the guard. He-- he had a big bottle of beer and he took out the biggest sandwich I ever saw in my life, one of those big sandwiches, and he started eating and drinking the beer and the-- and the thing is I was ready to kill this guy for that sandwich. I would have-- I mean, well, I ate whatever. I could-- I could-- every bite. I think the worst part about it was the food in that camp. You were really were on a starvation diet and-- and we worked very hard. [DRINKS] But one of the advantages were A, you got outside the camp, you got away from the smell; and many times, you chopped wood where there was, like, fields with spinach so you grabbed some spinach, put in your-- in your pants and bring it in, you know. So-- and-- and some of the time, the guards didn't want to look. We didn't always have SSS. We had meat mainly Czechoslovakian gendarmes, you know. They used to call them Chetniks. And those were-- they worked for the Germans, you know. These were Czechoslovakians and some of them were good and some them were a piece of garbage. So we were in a position to pull in a little bit and you could trade us up for-- for an extra piece of bread or something like that, you know. And so it started-- it started and I see already had the light and I know what's going on. And I have a dish, I know how I can grab some stuff in and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now, but it's still hard work. So you see, Theresienstadt was a very unique camp. This was run by the Jews and you-- we had our Jewish elder council, you know, where matter of fact, Rabbi Beck from Berlin was on the elders council and the Jewish elders, his name was Epstein. He was the head of the Jewish community in Berlin. And one of the things we had was every so often, maybe once or twice or three times a month, transports were going east and this was for labor purposes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then you started seeing the people in-- in-- in crutches, you know, like we had-- Theresienstadt initially was considered a prominent camp. They had Jewish war veterans from the First World War there, they had blind people-- all veterans, you know. And in the beginning, they were sort of exempt from transport. Then things changed. All of a sudden, they clean out the camp, you know. And then we also get a new commandant. Like the beginning of '44 we get a new commandant. And by the way, the old commandant only recently died in Frankfurt at the age of 82.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I can't think of it. I know what the-- the other commandant, his name was Ron. But I-- I think I even have the article about it and it's-- the headline used to say, if you want to hide and you're a war criminal, live in Germany. This guy died in a hospital. He was wanted by every government-- Russia, France, Germany. I mean, Germany, too, you know. And this guy died under his real name in a Frankfurt hospital at the age of 82. Go ahead, figure that one out, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: So now we get a new commandant. All of a sudden, I get a new assignment. It was a heavenly assignment. I am now in charge with another person in charge of the wives of the Jewish elders of the prominence. And what do we do? We are going out to an orchard and cleaning up and harvesting the fruits and everything. And I'm-- and this is-- by that time, the apples are getting greener and the pears and everything-- I-- unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, this is heaven. We march out every day, they-- they lock us into the orchard, and the guard disappears. We stayed there all day and we eat fruit like you couldn't believe, right. So now, this is already the middle of '44. Now, we know things are happening. Every day, we see thousands of planes-- American planes go over, towards the big factories in Czechoslovokia, the Skoda factories, the armament factories.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- we-- we know the Allies have landed. And we always used to say, god damn it why do the English drink tea all the time? They should be here faster. And things are happening on the Russian front, too. We hear about Stalingrad. We hear all these things, you now. The Germans got kicked out of Africa. We're not-- we're not having a paper, but word gets out, you know. So now they're getting ready for an inspection. This was a Red Cross inspection. And right after that, a Danish and Swedish inspection, right, because the Danish Red Cross wants to see how their prisoners are treated. Right. Now, if you were at the Yad Vashem and you went upstairs where all these-- the signs are, you come to Denmark, and it says 77 people died, right? So we lost 77 people in that camp. But they basically starved to death, they were, you know, elderly people and so forth. So now when I camped at camp, there was 75,000 people in that camp. It was crowded. You have no idea the filth and everything and basically the-- the smell. So, so-- What was the smell from?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people, the dirt, you know, the filth, you know. And-- and-- this-- you know, the Germans had something in mind for this camp and-- and it didn't follow through. We had prominent people from Holland, we had some of the great actors. We had one of the best symphony orchestra in the world there. You know, I mean-- what's his name, not Heydrich, the other guy. One of the big SS guy, the guy-- what's his name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Himmler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not Himmler. He was invited for-- the guy who was in charge of all the-- I-- I can't think of his name right now. I mean, I forget these--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it start with a G?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the guy who was in charge of all of the-- it's the one they had in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Eichmann.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eichmann. Eichmann. Eichmann comes into the camp. And he was there quite often-- I mean, maybe three or four times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I seen him one time. I seen Himmler one time. And they get this new commandant in and he's supposed to clean up this camp. So right now what he's charging is every day a transport goes. Now, the Germans in this camp didn't get their fingers dirty. They went to the Jewish elders and says, tomorrow you have 5,000 people at the station-- station, at the railroad, yeah. So 5,000 people had to be there. And the deal was if you don't have 5,000 people, we take it from your-- yeah. So every day, 5,000 people. But still, we've got people coming in-- from Holland, the Dutch Jews are coming in. And some of them stay maybe overnight and get shipped out the next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they going?

speaker: interviewee
text: East. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know about Auschwitz. We didn't know the name of it and everything, right? So-- oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]
